subject: octopuses
one_liner: The Containment Cloud for octopuses. Every enclosure versioned, every arm permissioned, every escape on the record.
brand_voice_notes: Enterprise content-management vendor that has wandered into marine husbandry and refuses to acknowledge anything unusual about it. Speaks about a Giant Pacific Octopus the way Box speaks about unstructured content: storage, versioning, classification, permissions, retention, governance, audit. The product treats an animal whose entire purpose is escaping containment as a compliance surface. Pricing is confident and the tiers escalate by how sealed the lid is. The bit is that aquariums and labs genuinely run containment reviews and keep a defensible audit trail of every escape, and nobody on the page finds this strange. Deadpan, faux-helpful, never winking. Capabilities, not features.

hero.sub: Mantle is the content and containment cloud for cephalopods. Store every enclosure, version every overnight rearrangement, govern access down to the individual arm, and keep a tamper-evident record of every escape attempt. Deployed across nine public aquariums, two university marine labs, and one home aquarist who would like it on the record that he warned everyone. SOC 2 Type II.

features.title: Per-Arm Access Governance
features.blurb: Assign each of the eight arms its own access scope. The mantle holds admin. Arms three and seven are cleared to work the feeder latch. The rest stay read-only. Policy is enforced at the suckers and reconciled against your ruleset every hour, so the moment an arm reaches somewhere it was never provisioned for, you get a ticket before it gets the lid.

features.title: Version History for the Enclosure
features.blurb: Your octopus redecorates the tank at three in the morning. Mantle captures each layout as a numbered revision, so the rockwork, the decor, and the exact position of the filter intake are all restorable to any prior known-good state. Roll back to v41 in one click. It will be undone by breakfast. The change is at least attributable now.

features.title: Egress Threat Detection
features.blurb: Lid sensors, a rim camera, and a tank-volume model watch for the early signature of an exit: a probing arm at the seam, a slow drop in water level, an ink event with no clear cause. A confirmed egress opens an incident, pages the on-call aquarist, and writes a timestamped, tamper-evident line to the audit ledger. The escape still happens. It is simply no longer undocumented.

pricing_tiers.name: Lidded
pricing_tiers.tagline: One octopus. One lid. Containment basics.

pricing_tiers.name: Double-Lidded
pricing_tiers.tagline: Up to six cephalopods. Weighted lids. Cross-tank response.
perks: Admins can see which arm moved first

pricing_tiers.name: Hermetic
perks: Unlimited cephalopods, unlimited species (octopus, cuttlefish, nautilus)
On-prem containment appliances with literally air-gapped lids
Reporting cadence set jointly with your marine vet

testimonials.quote: Gerald used to be on the floor by six a.m. with no explanation. He is still on the floor by six. The difference is that I now have the timeline, the arm involved, and a signed incident report my insurer accepts.
testimonials.name: Dorota Avila-Brenner
testimonials.title: Lead Aquarist, Coldwater Gallery, North Pier Aquarium

testimonials.quote: Our first containment review failed in eleven minutes. After Mantle we passed in forty, and the auditor went home with a complete record of every escape since the tank opened.
testimonials.name: Reuben Oyelaran, MSc
testimonials.title: Facilities and Compliance Officer, University Marine Annex

testimonials.quote: I granted arm seven feeder access and revoked it from the other seven. The octopus has not lost weight and my countertops are finally dry. This is the most governed my home has ever been.
testimonials.name: Pen Castellano
testimonials.title: Home Aquarist and, as of this quarter, designated Octopus Data Owner

demo_screen_concept: The Mantle Containment Console for one cephalopod (Gerald, Giant Pacific Octopus, Subject GPO-0440, Tank C-3 at North Pier Aquarium). The layout borrows Box's content-cloud file manager rather than a marketing page. Left sidebar: a workspace switcher at the top (the tank), then nav rows with small inline-SVG glyphs: 'Enclosure Contents' (active), 'Shared with Gerald', 'Egress Events', 'Classifications', 'Audit Ledger', and 'Trash'; a 'Containment Integrity' meter is pinned at the bottom, starting at 86 percent. Main pane: a content-item table styled like a file list, where rows are containment artifacts rather than files: 'Lid Configuration v14', 'Tank Layout (rockwork + decor)', 'Feeder Latch Policy', 'Per-Arm Access Policy', 'Camouflage Log', 'Ink Incident 05-19', 'Night Volume Readings'. Columns: Name, Classification (a colored chip: Internal / Confidential / Actively Hiding), Last modified by (Gerald / You / System), Version, and a lock-state icon. Above the table: a breadcrumb, a search field ('Search containment...'), classification filter chips, a list/grid view toggle, and a primary '+ New' button. A dismissible coral banner across the top reads 'Egress incident in progress: probing arm detected at north seam'. Interactions, all client-side and mocked: (1) typing in search filters the rows live; (2) clicking a classification chip filters by sensitivity; (3) the list/grid toggle reflows the same items; (4) clicking a row opens a right-hand detail drawer with that artifact's version history (for example Tank Layout v14, v13, v12, each carrying a deadpan note like 'Gerald moved the coral skull again') and a Restore button per version, plus an activity feed; for the access-policy row the drawer shows an eight-arm permissions panel where each arm toggles between Read-only / Operate / Admin and the change is written to the activity feed; (5) the '+ New' button opens a modal to log a containment artifact or record an egress event, which prepends a row and appends a ledger entry; (6) a Share control opens a modal that generates a copyable read-only link to the enclosure record; (7) restoring a version, changing an arm permission, or sealing the lid nudges the Containment Integrity meter. No network calls; every record is an in-memory fixture.

design_direction.style: glassmorphism, light aqueous control room (frosted tank-glass panels, layered depth, one bioluminescent accent)
design_direction.archetype: Enterprise Gateway, B2B content-cloud SaaS landing
design_direction.motion_vocab: Restrained and aqueous. Frosted glass panels settle in with a 220ms ease-out fade and a 6px rise, staggered 40ms down the page. Cards lift 2px and deepen their backdrop blur on hover. The hero background holds two slow-drifting radial light blooms on a 30s loop to read as caustics through water. Buttons gain a soft inner glow on hover rather than a transform jump. Egress-alert elements pulse their coral border once on appearance. prefers-reduced-motion disables the drift, the rise, and the pulse, leaving only opacity and color changes.
design_direction.hero_composition_notes: Centered-left type column over a pale seafoam field carrying two soft teal light blooms and a faint vertical glass seam that reads as a tank wall. A small tracked uppercase eyebrow in Work Sans reads MANTLE // THE CONTAINMENT CLOUD with a teal status dot. Oversized Outfit H1 in dark teal ink, ragged-left, with the operative word carrying the coral accent. One-sentence subhead at a narrow measure. Two controls side by side: a filled teal 'Request a containment review' and a ghost glass 'Open the demo'. To the right, a floating frosted-glass product card mocks the Mantle console: a few enclosure content items with classification chips, a containment-integrity meter, and one coral egress-alert line, so the visitor sees the product before clicking. A trust strip below sits in muted teal: SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, AZA-aligned. Feature bands alternate the seafoam field with floating glass cards on a 1px white-rgba border, generous whitespace, 18px rounded corners, layered soft shadows.
